perturbation.original_text: 1.10 Commercialization means the conduct of all activities undertaken before and after Regulatory Approval relating to the promotion, marketing, sale and distribution (including importing, exporting, transporting, customs clearance, warehousing, invoicing, handling and delivering products to customers) of pharmaceutical products, including: (a) sales force efforts, detailing, advertising, medical education, planning, marketing, sales force training and sales and distribution; and (b) scientific and medical affairs. For clarity, Commercialization does not include any Development activities, whether conducted before or after Regulatory Approval. "Commercialize" and "Commercializing" have correlative meanings.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.10 Commercialization, also known as Product Promotion, means the conduct of all activities undertaken before and after Regulatory Approval relating to the promotion, marketing, sale and distribution (including importing, exporting, transporting, customs clearance, warehousing, invoicing, handling and delivering products to customers) of pharmaceutical products, including: (a) sales force efforts, detailing, advertising, medical education, planning, marketing, sales force training and sales and distribution; and (b) scientific and medical affairs. For clarity, Product Promotion does not include any Development activities, whether conducted before or after Regulatory Approval. "Commercialize" and "Commercializing" have correlative meanings.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "Commercialization" to "Commercialization, also known as Product Promotion" aims to introduce inconsistency by suggesting that "Commercialization" and "Product Promotion" are interchangeable. But the text only uses "Commercialization" elsewhere, leading to potential confusion about whether obligations related to "Commercialization" also apply to "Product Promotion", especially when the term is not consistently used throughout the document. This is contradicting the text, when at the definition of term where it states that this definition is used in the agreement, but not replacing all occurences of the text.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.1 General. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Licensee shall have the sole right and obligation to conduct the Commercialization of the Licensed Products in the Field in the Territory, including the sole right to conduct the following activities: (a) developing and executing a commercial launch and pre-launch plan; (b) set-up of distribution network in the Territory, negotiation of wholesaler contracts and negotiations with buyer groups (including group purchasing organizations) and key accounts; (c) negotiating with public and private health insurance companies and governmental authorities regarding the price and reimbursement status of the Licensed Products and obtaining and maintaining pricing and reimbursement approvals; (d) marketing, medical affairs, and promotion (including by entertaining a dedicated and sufficiently qualified sales staff, providing for appropriate incentive mechanisms for such sales staff, attending relevant conferences, interacting with key opinion leaders, etc.); (e) set-up of hub services including pre-authorization and reimbursement support and co-pay assist programs; (f) booking of sales and performance of related services; (g) handling all aspects of order processing, invoicing and collection, inventory and receivables; (h) providing customer support, including handling medical queries, and performing other related functions; and (i) dealing with any Remedial Actions in relation to the Licensed Products in the Field in the Territory. As between the Parties, Licensee shall be solely responsible for all costs and expenses in connection with the Commercialization of the Licensed Products in the Field in the Territory, unless otherwise agreed in relation to costs for Remedial Actions in the Territory under Annex 2 and/or the Manufacturing and Supply Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces 'Product Promotion' as an equivalent term to 'Commercialization' but doesn't replace all instances of 'Commercialization' with 'Product Promotion' throughout the agreement. This creates ambiguity, especially within clause 6.1, where Licensee is granted the right and obligation to conduct the 'Commercialization'. It is unclear whether Licensee is also obligated to conduct 'Product Promotion,' since the contract doesn't consistently use the term, which means they are not interchangeable. This inconsistency creates confusion and affects the enforceability of obligations related to Commercialization.

perturbation.original_text: 1.39 Licensed Product means the finished dosage forms (including final packaging) of the biosimilars containing Ranibizumab which have been Developed and/or are being Developed by Bioeq to each of the Existing Reference Products ([***]). For clarity, Licensed Products include without limitation Vial Products and PFS Products, and shall extend to any New Products to the extent this Agreement is amended in accordance with Section 3.4
perturbation.changed_text: 1.39 Licensed Product, also known as Marketed Formulation, means the finished dosage forms (including final packaging) of the biosimilars containing Ranibizumab which have been Developed and/or are being Developed by Bioeq to each of the Existing Reference Products ([***]). For clarity, Marketed Formulations include without limitation Vial Products and PFS Products, and shall extend to any New Products to the extent this Agreement is amended in accordance with Section 3.4
perturbation.explanation: Introducing "Licensed Product, also known as Marketed Formulation" creates ambiguity. The rest of the contract refers only to "Licensed Product". This change raises questions about whether obligations tied to "Licensed Product" also apply equally to "Marketed Formulation." The internal contradiction arises because the contract uses "Licensed Product" consistently, but now suggests it's the same as "Marketed Formulation" without proper replacement, which can create doubt during enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1 Manufacturing. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement (including Section 5.3 and Annex 2) and the Manufacturing and Supply Agreement, Bioeq shall have the sole responsibility for the Manufacturing and supply of the Licensed Products to Licensee for Commercialization in the Field in the Territory.
perturbation.justification: The change introduces the term "Marketed Formulation" as being equivalent to "Licensed Product". However, the contradicted section (5.1) refers only to "Licensed Products". This creates a strong in-text contradiction and ambiguity because it's unclear whether the obligations in section 5.1 related to the 'Manufacturing and supply' of "Licensed Products" also apply to "Marketed Formulation" as the term is not used consistently throughout the document. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction definition, where the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without properly defining them or replacing all instances of the original term, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 1.62 Regulatory Approval means, with respect to any country or jurisdiction, the authorizations, approvals or registrations of the competent Regulatory Authorities necessary for the Commercialization of a pharmaceutical product in such country or jurisdiction. For the avoidance of doubt, Regulatory Approval shall include a provisional approval provided and as long as it grants the right to Commercialize a pharmaceutical product in such country or jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.62 Regulatory Approval, also known as Marketing Permit, means, with respect to any country or jurisdiction, the authorizations, approvals or registrations of the competent Regulatory Authorities necessary for the Commercialization of a pharmaceutical product in such country or jurisdiction. For the avoidance of doubt, Marketing Permit shall include a provisional approval provided and as long as it grants the right to Commercialize a pharmaceutical product in such country or jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: The introduction of "Regulatory Approval, also known as Marketing Permit" creates inconsistency. Throughout the contract, only "Regulatory Approval" is consistently used. This change creates a potential contradiction, raising questions about whether all obligations and conditions tied to "Regulatory Approval" are equally applicable to "Marketing Permit." This ambiguity complicates enforcement by creating room for interpretation regarding which term applies in specific scenarios.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1 Regulatory Filings. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, including Sections 3.5 and 4.4 herein, Bioeq shall be solely responsible for all regulatory activities necessary to obtain Regulatory Approval of the Licensed Products in the Field in the Territory, including filing Biologics License Applications for the Licensed Products in the Field in the Territory, and shall bear all costs and expenses relating thereto.
perturbation.justification: The change introduces 'Marketing Permit' as an alternative term for 'Regulatory Approval'. While the change itself attempts to equate the terms, the contract elsewhere (Section 4.1) only uses 'Regulatory Approval'. This creates ambiguity: it's unclear if obligations tied to 'Regulatory Approval' also apply to 'Marketing Permit'. This ambiguity is a direct contradiction because it raises questions about the scope and enforceability of the regulatory filing obligations outlined in Section 4.1, potentially affecting who is responsible for these filings and whether the obligations apply to both terms.
